content.text: Trace and Log Retention Tier Policies

Every log line and every trace span you emit has a cost: storage fees, query scan costs, and the latency overhead of indexing. Left unchecked, a Claude application emitting structured logs at 100 requests per second accumulates gigabytes per day across app logs, API spans, infra metrics, and audit records. Without an explicit tier policy, operations teams either pay for hot storage indefinitely or lose historical data entirely when they eventually panic-delete to save costs.

A tiered retention policy separates the four distinct concerns: operational debugging (hot, fast, recent), capacity and compliance analysis (warm, queryable), regulatory evidence (cold, archived), and expired data (deleted, with audit). Each tier has distinct query-latency guarantees, storage costs per GB, and legal retention requirements. This document defines the tier structure for Claude application telemetry and shows how to implement lifecycle transitions as code.

attrs.alt: Trace and log retention tier policies — hot, warm, cold, and deletion zones

content.text: Telemetry data has a predictable value curve over time: it is queried constantly in the first 24 hours after emission, occasionally in the first month, rarely in the first year, and almost never after that — except for compliance queries. The tier model exploits this by moving data to cheaper storage as it ages.

if you are paying hot-tier rates for data older than 30 days, you are almost certainly wasting money. Audit logs are the one exception — they stay warm or cold indefinitely to satisfy regulatory requirements, but never need hot-tier query speed.
Not all log types age at the same rate. Define explicit retention windows per data category and document them in your Data Processing Addendum (DPA) if you handle user data.

The 7-year floor for audit logs is driven by SOC 2 Type II requirements and most EU/US financial compliance frameworks. If your organisation operates under HIPAA, the minimum is 6 years; under PCI-DSS it is 1 year for most records but 3 years for cardholder-environment logs. Use whichever is longest.

AWS provides two independent lifecycle mechanisms: CloudWatch Logs retention policies (for logs ingested directly) and S3 Lifecycle rules (for logs exported to S3). Use both: set a short CloudWatch retention to avoid paying Logs storage rates beyond the hot window, and an S3 lifecycle rule to transition exported logs through Standard → Glacier → expiry.
Grafana Loki's compactor handles retention by deleting chunks whose age exceeds the configured window. Set retention per stream using label matchers so different log types expire at different rates.

# loki.yaml — retention configuration (Loki >= 2.8)
compactor:
  working_directory: /var/loki/compactor
  shared_store: s3
  compaction_interval: 10m
  retention_enabled: true
  retention_delete_delay: 2h        # grace period before actual deletion
  retention_delete_worker_count: 150

limits_config:
  retention_period: 720h            # global default: 30 days

# Per-stream overrides (label-based)
# Requires per_tenant_override_config or ruler-style overrides:
per_tenant_override_config: /etc/loki/overrides.yaml

# /etc/loki/overrides.yaml
overrides:
  prod:
    ingestion_rate_mb: 64
    retention_period: 720h     # 30d default for this tenant

# To set per-stream retention, use Loki's stream selector retention:
# (Loki >= 2.9 with ruler retention rules)
retention_rules:
  - selector: '{log_type="audit"}'
    period: 2160h    # 90 days warm before Loki itself deletes
  - selector: '{log_type="infra"}'
    period: 336h     # 14 days hot only
  - selector: '{log_type="app"}'
    period: 720h     # 30 days

Prometheus keeps metrics in a local TSDB with a configurable retention window. For a Claude application, the Prometheus TSDB should hold at most 30 days of raw scrapes. Long-term metric history belongs in a remote write target (Thanos, Mimir, Cortex) where it is stored cheaply as object-store blocks.

# prometheus.yml — retention settings
global:
  scrape_interval: 15s
  evaluation_interval: 15s

# Local TSDB: keep 30 days
# CLI flag: --storage.tsdb.retention.time=30d
# Or equivalently in the Helm chart:
server:
  retention: "30d"
  retentionSize: "50GB"     # also cap by size — whichever triggers first

# Remote write to Thanos or Mimir for long-term (warm/cold tier for metrics)
remote_write:
  - url: "http://thanos-receive:19291/api/v1/receive"
    queue_config:
      max_samples_per_send: 10000
      batch_send_deadline: 5s
    write_relabel_configs:
      # Drop high-cardinality debug metrics from remote — keep only SLO-relevant series
      - source_labels: [__name__]
        regex: "go_gc_.*|process_.*"
        action: drop

Distributed traces are expensive: a single Claude API call that fans out through auth, rate-limiting, prompt construction, and response streaming may generate 50–200 spans. Storing every span from every request is rarely useful and quickly unaffordable. The standard approach is probabilistic head-based sampling combined with tail-based sampling for errors.
Head-Based Sampling Rates by Traffic Tier
Abundant data, not needed for debugging

# Grafana Tempo retention (tempo.yaml)
compactor:
  compaction:
    block_retention: 720h     # 30 days for sampled traces
    compacted_block_retention: 1h

# For error traces, use a separate tenant with longer retention:
# Override per tenant in multi-tenant mode:
overrides:
  error-traces-tenant:
    block_retention: 2160h    # 90 days for error-only trace tenant

Manual lifecycle configuration drifts. A storage bucket created last year by a contractor may have no lifecycle rule. Automate compliance checking as a daily job that enumerates all log storage resources and verifies each has a lifecycle rule matching policy.

# .github/workflows/retention-compliance.yaml
name: Retention Compliance Check
on:
  schedule:
    - cron: "0 6 * * *"   # daily at 06:00 UTC
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  check:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      id-token: write
      contents: read
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Configure AWS credentials
        uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: arn:aws:iam::123456789:role/retention-checker
          aws-region: us-east-1
      - name: Run retention compliance check
        run: python3 scripts/check-retention-compliance.py

Before finalising retention windows, estimate the steady-state storage cost. The following formula gives the monthly GB stored in each tier assuming constant daily ingest and no compression (real-world compression ratios for JSON logs typically reach 5–10x with gzip).
= GB in that tier at steady state. Multiply by the per-tier cost to get monthly spend.

# Retention cost estimator for a Claude application
# Adjust daily_ingest_gb_per_type to match your Prometheus metrics.

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class LogType:
    name: str
    daily_ingest_gb: float
    hot_days: int
    warm_days: int
    cold_days: int
    compression_ratio: float = 6.0  # JSON logs typically 5-10x gzip

HOT_COST  = 0.30   # $/GB/month (CloudWatch / Loki hot)
WARM_COST = 0.023  # $/GB/month (S3 Standard)
COLD_COST = 0.004  # $/GB/month (S3 Glacier)

LOG_TYPES = [
    LogType("app-logs",     daily_ingest_gb=5.0,  hot_days=30, warm_days=60,  cold_days=0),
    LogType("audit-logs",   daily_ingest_gb=0.1,  hot_days=30, warm_days=60,  cold_days=2555),
    LogType("infra-logs",   daily_ingest_gb=2.0,  hot_days=14, warm_days=46,  cold_days=0),
    LogType("traces",       daily_ingest_gb=3.0,  hot_days=7,  warm_days=23,  cold_days=0),
]

total_monthly_usd = 0.0
print(f"{'Type':<14} {'Hot GB':>8} {'Warm GB':>8} {'Cold GB':>8} {'$/mo':>8}")
print("-" * 54)
for lt in LOG_TYPES:
    compressed = lt.daily_ingest_gb / lt.compression_ratio
    hot_gb  = compressed * lt.hot_days
    warm_gb = compressed * lt.warm_days
    cold_gb = compressed * lt.cold_days
    cost = hot_gb * HOT_COST + warm_gb * WARM_COST + cold_gb * COLD_COST
    total_monthly_usd += cost
    print(f"{lt.name:<14} {hot_gb:>8.1f} {warm_gb:>8.1f} {cold_gb:>8.1f} {cost:>7.2f}")

print("-" * 54)
print(f"{'TOTAL':<14} {'':>8} {'':>8} {'':>8} {total_monthly_usd:>7.2f}")

With 5.0 GB/day of app logs and 6x compression the steady-state hot-tier store is 25 GB (5 GB/day × 30 days / 6 = 25 GB). At $0.30/GB/month that is $7.50/month for app logs alone in the hot tier — a tractable number that makes the case for exporting to S3 for the warm window rather than leaving it in Loki.

Tiered retention policies interact with GDPR Article 17 right-to-erasure. When a user requests deletion, you must be able to purge their data from every tier — including cold Glacier archives. The practical approach is to avoid logging user-identifying data in the first place (see PII scrubbing), but for audit logs that reference a hashed user ID, you need a mapping table and a deletion procedure.
delete by Loki stream selector or CloudWatch filter. Takes minutes.
identify and delete S3 objects by prefix/tag. Takes hours for large buckets.
Glacier restore → identify → overwrite/delete → re-archive. Can take days. Document the SLA.
audit logs referencing a user for a past compliance event may be exempt from erasure under legitimate interest — confirm with legal before deleting.
sampled traces rarely contain user IDs after PII scrubbing; verify before promising erasure.
Document your erasure SLA in your Privacy Policy. A common commitment is 30 days for hot/warm and 90 days for cold tier — the cold restore latency is the binding constraint.
A tiered retention policy is how you balance the competing demands of fast operational queries, long-term compliance, and storage cost for Claude application telemetry. The key principles:
Define explicit windows per data type — do not use a single global retention setting.
Move data from hot to warm automatically via lifecycle rules, not manual scripts.
Audit logs go to cold tier (Glacier) and stay there for 7 years minimum.
Traces are the most expensive data type per GB — sample aggressively and retain briefly.
Compress all logs before writing to object storage (6–10x ratio for JSON is realistic).
Automate compliance checks daily — unmanaged buckets will drift outside policy.
Document your GDPR erasure SLA against each tier; cold tier restore sets the ceiling.
